text.title: Wave parallelism and worktree isolation
text.summary: The orchestrator groups tickets into execution waves based on file overlap, and runs each wave in isolated git worktrees so concurrent edits never collide on the main working tree.

section.title: Wave admission rules
section.p: Two tickets may run in the same wave if any of the following holds:

list.item: They live in the same repository but touch disjoint files.
They live in the same repository and same file, but at non-overlapping line ranges, AND the orchestrator accepts a possible rebase between them.

section.p: Otherwise the tickets are placed in sequential waves. When in doubt, default to a separate worktree per ticket.

rule.statement: Every parallel executor subagent works inside its own `git worktree add -b ` and removes the worktree after merging back.
rule.rationale: The main working tree often carries uncommitted local-only state (preview data, dev toggles). Concurrent agents touching it would corrupt each other. Worktrees give each agent a clean, branch-scoped checkout sharing the same `.git/` for cheap branching.

example.code: git -C <repo> worktree add /tmp/wt-<ticket-id> -b feature-<ticket-id>-<slug> <default-branch> cd /tmp/wt-<ticket-id> # work, commit on feature branch cd <repo> && git checkout <default-branch> && git merge feature-<ticket-id>-<slug> --no-edit git worktree remove /tmp/wt-<ticket-id> git branch -d feature-<ticket-id>-<slug>

rule.statement: An executor that hits a merge or rebase conflict MUST stop, list conflict files plus both sides' intent, and return control to the orchestrator. The resolver subagent is then dispatched.
rule.rationale: Auto-resolving with `--ours` / `--theirs` silently destroys one side. Conflict resolution is its own task with its own prompt file, not a side effect of execution.

list.item: Reads the feature spec/design to understand the current change's intent.
Reads `git log` and `git show` for the conflicting file to understand the other side's intent.
Combines both intents at the line level, with a documented decision per file.
Special-cases append-only files (release notes, changelogs, indexes) by keeping entries from both sides under the same heading.
